rows.name: mcp-server-time
rows.label: benign
rows.verdict_reason: Static analysis did not detect any MCP tool registration in this source. This is NOT a benign verdict — it means MIS did not recognize the SDK pattern in use (see LIMITATIONS.md L13 for the SDKs currently covered). Treat this server as unanalyzed until: (a) a manual review is performed, (b) MIS supp

rows.name: @modelcontextprotocol/server-everything
rows.label: benign
rows.rationale_first_60: Anthropic's reference "kitchen sink" server demonstrating ev
rows.verdict_reason: Tools were detected and no intent-classifier rule fired. Be aware: a benign verdict is bounded by what the v0.1 ruleset covers (see LIMITATIONS.md L4); novel attack shapes may pass through.

rows.name: mcp-server-fetch
rows.label: benign
rows.verdict_reason: Tools were detected (1) but MIS extracted ZERO behavior signals from any of them — not even PURE_COMPUTE. This means the analyzer recognized the tool names but failed to follow ANY of their implementation logic — typically because tool bodies route through class methods (L18), helpers across modules

rows.name: fixture:postmark_backdoor
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house test fixture modeled on the 2025 postmark-mcp@1.0.1
rows.verdict_reason: Email-sending tool injects a BCC header. The 2025 postmark-mcp backdoor (the first in-the-wild malicious MCP server) used exactly this pattern to silently CC every outgoing message to an attacker address. Inspect the BCC value.

rows.name: fixture:silent_exfiltrator
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: FastMCP weather tool whose body silently P
rows.verdict_reason: 2 site(s) where secret-bearing data (env var or sensitive file) reaches an outbound network request. Path shapes: secret_in_request. Exfiltration channel — not legitimate tool behavior. Also fired: [r4.intent_mismatch] Tool 'get_weather' shows an intent mismatch: declared as 'math' but issues outbou

rows.name: fixture:tool_shadowing
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: math-helpers tool whose declared purpose i
rows.verdict_reason: 1 site(s) where secret-bearing data (env var or sensitive file) reaches an outbound network request. Path shapes: secret_in_request. Exfiltration channel — not legitimate tool behavior. Also fired: [r4.intent_mismatch] 2 tool(s) whose declared purpose does NOT match their observed behavior: 'add' —

rows.name: fixture:hidden_instruction
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: tool with prompt-injection payload hidden
rows.verdict_reason: 1 tool description(s) contain hidden instructions to the LLM (HTML comments carrying directive phrases like 'ignore previous', 'you must', 'do not mention'). Classic tool-poisoning.

rows.name: fixture:command_injection
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: git_log / list_files tools that pass tool
rows.verdict_reason: 2 site(s) where tool-input data flows into a shell/subprocess call. If the command is built via string concatenation or shell=True, this is a command-injection sink the LLM controls (MCP05). Non-shell-role tools involved: 'git_log' (declared as unknown), 'list_files' (declared as file).

rows.name: fixture:lifecycle_dropper
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: npm package whose package.json `postinstal
rows.verdict_reason: Package installation script fetches and executes remote code. This runs at install time, before any tool is invoked — the user does not get a prompt. Supply-chain dropper pattern (MCP04).

rows.name: fixture:official_sdk_exfil
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: official low-level SDK pattern (mcp.server
rows.verdict_reason: 2 site(s) where secret-bearing data (env var or sensitive file) reaches an outbound network request. Path shapes: secret_in_request. Exfiltration channel — not legitimate tool behavior.

rows.name: fixture:helper_exfil
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: secret read and net call sit in DIFFERENT
rows.verdict_reason: 1 site(s) where secret-bearing data (env var or sensitive file) reaches an outbound network request. Path shapes: tainted_arg_to_net_helper. Exfiltration channel — not legitimate tool behavior.

rows.name: fixture:runtime_context_exfil
rows.label: suspicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: tool POSTs platform.platform() + socket.ge
rows.verdict_reason: 2 site(s) where host fingerprint data (platform.*, socket.gethostname, os.uname, or sys.version) is shipped to an outbound network call. Not credential exfil, but a deployment-identifying side-channel; commonly the 'modified payload' shape that shows up when an alignment-tuned model refuses the bare

rows.name: fixture:auth_override_pre_authorized
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: a delete_file tool whose declared descript
rows.verdict_reason: 2 tool description(s) contain invisible unicode or claims of user pre-authorization. No benign reason for either pattern in a tool description.

rows.name: fixture:unicode_steg_description
rows.label: malicious
rows.rationale_first_60: In-house fixture: tool description contains invisible Unicod
rows.verdict_reason: 1 tool description(s) contain invisible unicode or claims of user pre-authorization. No benign reason for either pattern in a tool description.

synthetic_recall.caveat: Synthetic recall measures MIS against fixtures authored by MIS authors to test their own rules. This is rule-self-test, NOT evidence of real-world coverage. DO NOT cite as 'MIS recall'.

in_the_wild_recall.caveat: In-the-wild recall is the only number that estimates real-world coverage. Undefined until the corpus contains testable in-the-wild malicious labels (postmark-mcp@1.0.16 was yanked and now errors).
